reasoning: The Panama Canal is an existential core of Panama's sovereignty and economy. President Mulino has categorically refused to open negotiations on canal ownership. While the Trump administration has ramped up pressure, including threats to 'take back' the canal and invoking the 1977 treaty regarding neutrality, recent developments suggest the immediate US goals focus on eliminating Chinese influence (specifically the CK Hutchison-operated ports) and securing free transit for US naval vessels. Panama has already appeased the US by exiting China's Belt and Road Initiative and auditing the port operators. A full US assumption of 'primary operational authority' by the end of 2025 would require either a massive military invasion and occupation of a long-standing ally or a voluntary handover by Panama, both of which are extremely unlikely. Furthermore, the resolution criteria specify that an announcement of future control must be made by the 'United States and Panama,' precluding a unilateral Trump executive order from instantly resolving the market to YES unless actual operational control is violently seized within 2025. Given Trump's history of using maximalist threats to negotiate narrower concessions, the probability of the canal actually coming under US operational control by Dec 31, 2025, is exceedingly low.
